plainMeaning: For a long, wonder-stirring time the two Jinas ruled as kings over an ocean-bounded earth, where their elephants, horses and thrones came the instant they were wished for and graceful women filled the realm with sweet song.
recitation: jaladhi rasanāvanī vitata vara śāsanaṁ, cintitopasthita dvirada turagāsanam | lalita lalanā janābaddha bahu barkaraṁ, yau ciraṁ rājyam avataḥ sma vismayakaram ||

wordByWord.etymology: jala (water) + dhi (holder/receptacle) = 'water-holder', the sea; first member of the compound 'jaladhi-rasanā-avanī'.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the bahuvrīhi describing the extent of the realm.

wordByWord.gloss: the earth girdled (by the ocean) — rasanā (girdle/belt) + avanī (earth)
wordByWord.etymology: rasanā 'waist-girdle' + avanī 'earth'; with the preceding jaladhi, 'the earth whose girdle is the ocean' = the ocean-bounded world.
wordByWord.notes: Vowel-sandhi rasanā + avanī → rasanāvanī. A stock kāvya image for 'the whole earth'.
Their rule is 'vitata' over the ocean-girdled earth.

wordByWord.etymology: √vṛ (to choose); 'that which is chosen' → best, excellent.
√śās (to command, instruct, rule) + -ana; accusative singular, agreeing with rājyam.
wordByWord.notes: Here secular sovereignty, not the doctrinal sense of jina-śāsana.

wordByWord.gloss: presenting itself the moment it is thought of
wordByWord.etymology: cintita (thought of, √cint) + upasthita (arrived, standing near; upa + √sthā), joined by sandhi cintita + upasthita.
wordByWord.notes: A wish-fulfilling motif: whatever the kings desired appeared at once.

wordByWord.etymology: dvi (two) + rada (tusk/tooth, √rad 'to scrape'); literally 'two-tusked', a synonym of gaja.
wordByWord.notes: Royal war-mounts; first of the trio elephant–horse–throne.

wordByWord.gloss: horses and thrones/seats — turaga (horse) + āsana (seat, throne)
wordByWord.etymology: turaga 'swift-goer' (tura, speed + √gam, to go) + āsana 'seat' (√ās, to sit); sandhi turaga + āsanam. The whole 'cintitopasthita-dvirada-turaga-āsanam' is a bahuvrīhi qualifying rājyam.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative singular; lists the elephants, horses and thrones that materialise on a wish.

wordByWord.etymology: √lal (to fondle, play) + -anā; 'one who is dallied with / graceful one' → woman.
wordByWord.notes: Note the chiming yamaka lalita lalanā, sound-play typical of this hymn.

wordByWord.gloss: thronged with / composed by the folk — jana (people) + ābaddha (bound to, thronged, composed)
wordByWord.etymology: jana 'people, folk' + ābaddha 'bound, fastened, formed' (ā + √bandh). Distinguish ābaddha (with long ā, 'bound/composed') from abaddha ('unbound').
wordByWord.notes: With lalita-lalanā: 'thronged with graceful womenfolk', and (ābaddha as 'composed') the song they make up.

wordByWord.etymology: Onomatopoeic/poetic, akin to barbara 'murmur, babble'; accusative singular agreeing with rājyam.
wordByWord.notes: Reading uncertain — see notes. Taken as the pleasant murmur/song of the womenfolk filling the realm; flagged as a possible book-specific spelling.

wordByWord.etymology: yad (relative pronoun), nominative masculine dual yau.
wordByWord.notes: Refers to Ajita and Śānti as the inseparable pair; correlative of the whole sentence.
Stresses the duration of their reigns.

wordByWord.etymology: rājan (king) + -ya; 'kingship, realm'; accusative singular, the object the three preceding bahuvrīhis qualify. In the book printed sandhi-joined as રાજ્યમવત:સ્મ (rājyam avataḥ sma).
wordByWord.notes: The grammatical hub of the verse: everything describes this rājya.

wordByWord.etymology: √av 'to protect, govern, favour'; dual past form, with sma giving habitual/narrative past sense. Sandhi-joined in the book as part of રાજ્યમવત:સ્મ.
wordByWord.notes: 'av' here unites the senses 'to rule' and 'to protect' — fitting for righteous kings.

wordByWord.etymology: Enclitic sma; appended to a verb to mark continued/narrated past, 'used to, did (long)'.
wordByWord.notes: With avataḥ: 'long did rule and protect'.

wordByWord.etymology: vismaya 'astonishment' (vi + √smi, to wonder) + kara 'making' (√kṛ); 'wonder-making', accusative qualifying rājyam.
wordByWord.notes: Caps the verse: a reign so splendid it amazed all who beheld it.

literalTranslation: The two [Jinas] for long ruled — and amazingly so — a wonder-causing kingdom: whose excellent governance extended over the ocean-girdled earth; whose elephants, horses and thrones stood present the moment they were thought of; thronged with graceful womenfolk and full of much sweet murmuring.
idiomaticTranslation: Long, and to the wonder of all, the two reigned as kings over an earth girdled by the seas, their rule stretching to its furthest shores. Elephants, horses and thrones rose up at the mere thought of them; lovely women thronged the court and filled it with sweet song. Such was the marvel-working kingdom these two long held and guarded.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse turns from the two Jinas' birth and bodily marks to the second of the five auspicious phases — their princely life before renunciation — by celebrating the sheer magnificence of their reigns. Three bahuvrīhi compounds pile splendour upon splendour onto a single noun, rājyam (kingdom), before the sentence resolves in the verb avataḥ sma ('the two long ruled and guarded'). First, scope: 'jaladhi-rasanāvanī-vitata-vara-śāsanaṁ' — their excellent rule reached across the whole earth, imagined as a lady whose girdle is the encircling ocean, the standard kāvya figure for universal sovereignty (and, for Śānti, a quiet nod to his cakravartin status). Second, ease and abundance: 'cintitopasthita-dvirada-turagāsanam' — war-elephants, horses and thrones present themselves the very instant they are wished for, the wish-fulfilling note that marks a Jina-to-be's merit. Third, courtly beauty: 'lalita-lalanā-janābaddha-bahu-barkaraṁ' — the realm is thronged with graceful women and alive with much sweet murmuring song. The dual relative 'yau' keeps Ajita and Śānti yoked as one even while describing two distinct ancient reigns, and 'vismayakaram' frames the whole as something that stirs wonder. Note the deliberate chiming — 'lalita lalanā', 'rasanā… vismaya' — the verse is built to be heard. Crucially, the hymn lingers on this grandeur only to make the next verse's renunciation more striking: all this they will soon give away in the vārṣika-dāna and walk into homelessness.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse illustrates the Jain teaching that a Tīrthaṅkara-to-be enjoys vast puṇya-phala (fruit of merit) — wish-fulfilling abundance and unchallenged dominion — yet this very plenty is the foil for vairāgya (dispassion). Worldly sovereignty, however 'wonder-causing', is impermanent and binding; its function in the pañca-kalyāṇaka narrative is precisely to be renounced. The pairing of bhoga (here, the splendid reign) with the imminent tyāga (renunciation in v.9) embodies the principle that the highest souls taste the best of saṁsāra and still turn away from it.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited devotionally, the verse invites the worshipper to admire the Jinas' worldly glory only as a stepping-stone: if those who possessed everything chose to let it go, the reciter is gently moved to loosen attachment to their own far smaller holdings. It reframes 'wonder' away from wealth and toward the renunciation that follows.

crossReferences.text: Pañca-kalyāṇaka — the rājya (princely reign) preceding dīkṣā in every Tīrthaṅkara's life
crossReferences.source: Standard Śvetāmbara biographies; cf. Kalpasūtra life-pattern

crossReferences.text: Śāntinātha as cakravartin ruling the ocean-girdled earth
Next verse (v.9): vārṣika-dāna and renunciation, to which this reign is the prelude

sources.title: Dvirada, Dvi-rada: 16 definitions — Wisdom Library
sources.consultedFor: Confirming dvirada = 'two-tusked', an elephant (dvi + rada), and its use as a synonym of gaja.

sources.title: Lalana — Wisdom Library
sources.consultedFor: Confirming lalanā = 'woman, graceful lady' (√lal), and the sense of lalita 'sportive, lovely'.

sources.title: द्विरद — KST Online Sanskrit Dictionary (kosha.sanskrit.today)
sources.consultedFor: Cross-checking the lexical entry and etymology of dvirada (elephant).

sources.title: Lalana — sanskritdictionary.org
sources.consultedFor: Secondary confirmation of lalanā ('woman, damsel') glosses across classical usage.

notes: Parsing is secure for three of the four padas: jaladhi-rasanā-avanī ('ocean-girdled earth'), cintitopasthita-dvirada-turagāsanam ('elephants/horses/thrones arriving on a wish'), and yau ciraṁ rājyam avataḥ sma vismayakaram ('the two long ruled the wonder-causing kingdom'); avataḥ is taken from √av in its dual sense 'protect/rule' with the past-marking particle sma. The one genuine uncertainty is બર્કરં (barkaraṁ): it is not a common classical lexeme. It is read here as an onomatopoeic 'sweet murmur/song' (cf. barbara 'babble'), parallel to the womenfolk of the same pāda; a variant could intend abundance or a book-specific orthography (the source book preserves its own readings, per its fidelity convention). The accusative -aṁ matches rājyam, so whatever its sense it qualifies the kingdom. ābaddha is taken with long ā ('thronged/composed with'), not abaddha ('unbound'). The metrical/orthographic joins (rasanā+avanī, turaga+āsanam, rājyam+avataḥ+sma) are sandhi within the verbatim book text and have been split for glossing only. Authorship of this Bṛhad version is left unattributed per the tradition knowledge base.
